summary: Gruber reflects on the iPad 2 introduction event, noting how it mirrored the original iPad launch in structure but differed profoundly in Apple's confidence and understanding of the product. He observes that Apple has shifted from positioning the iPad as a PC alternative to declaring it a 'post-PC device,' with demo apps moving from productivity (iWork) to creative play (iMovie, GarageBand). GarageBand for iPad is singled out as a transformative app that turns the device into something entirely new rather than a lesser Mac. Gruber argues that Apple's competitors fail not on technical grounds but on understanding the emotional, experiential dimension of products — symbolized by the simple choice of an armchair for on-stage demos.
key_points: Apple's understanding of the iPad evolved from 'we think this is good' to 'we know this is enormous' in just one year
The shift from iWork demos to iMovie and GarageBand reflects Apple repositioning the iPad from PC alternative to post-PC creative device
GarageBand for iPad represents the iPad doing something new that couldn't be done on a Mac, not just doing Mac tasks better
Apple's competitive advantages include non-technical elements — design, emotional appeal, retail experience — that competitors don't even recognize they should copy
Steve Jobs's unheralded entrance and the raucous ovation he received underscored the emotional weight of his return during his medical leave
money_quotes: Last year Apple didn't yet understand the iPad. They knew it was good. They knew it had potential. But they didn't know what it was.
GarageBand isn't a musical app running on an iPad. It turns an iPad into a musical instrument.
Good iPad apps can make the iPad feel not like a device running an app, but like an object that is the app.
GarageBand isn't the iPad doing something better than the Mac. This is the iPad doing something new, things that couldn't be done on the Mac.

Take that chair. The on-stage demos of the iPad aren't conducted at a table or a lectern. They're conducted sitting in an armchair. That conveys something about the feel of the iPad before its screen is even turned on.
How it feels is the entirety of the iPad's appeal.
It's a shame, almost, that we squandered the term 'personal computer' 30 years ago.
There are other things any competitor could copy, easily, but they seemingly don't even understand that they should, because such things aren't technical.
key_insight: Apple's deepest competitive advantage with the iPad isn't technical but experiential — competitors can't copy what they don't even recognize matters.
